AI.tldr: This paper proposes two fully decoupled, linear, structure-preserving block-centered finite difference schemes for the Keller-Segel chemotaxis system on staggered non-uniform grids, achieving second-order spatial accuracy and first/second-order temporal accuracy while preserving positivity, mass conservation, and energy dissipation.
AI.motivation: To develop numerical schemes for the Keller-Segel chemotaxis system that are both accurate and preserve essential physical properties (positivity, mass conservation, energy dissipation) at the discrete level, especially for simulating rapid blow-up phenomena on non-uniform grids.
AI.method: Two fully decoupled, linear, structure-preserving block-centered finite difference schemes on staggered non-uniform spatial grids; one scheme is first-order in time with unconditional positivity preservation, mass conservation, and energy dissipation, while the other is second-order in time with conditional positivity under a time-step constraint.
AI.result: Both schemes achieve second-order spatial accuracy; the first-order temporal scheme preserves positivity, mass conservation, and energy dissipation unconditionally; the second-order temporal scheme ensures positivity under a sufficient time-step condition; numerical experiments validate accuracy and reliability, especially for rapid blow-up simulations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed fully decoupled, linear schemes provide more accurate and efficient simulations of chemotactic dynamics on non-uniform grids, with strong theoretical guarantees on physical property preservation.

title: Strict 2.5D Shadows for One-Component Navier-Stokes Regularity
summary: We formulate and prove a conditional finite-scale reduction theorem for the local one-component regularity problem for suitable weak solutions of the three-dimensional Navier--Stokes equations. Starting from a scale-invariant bound Phi(1) <= M and smallness of the critical vertical component C_3(1) = delta, the argument compares the solution with a strict two-and-a-half-dimensional shadow class. The comparison is made in the harmonic-pressure quotient, which is the natural local topology for pressure compactness. The Reynolds commutator produced by coarse graining is treated as a positive covariance stress and is absorbed by an unresolved-variance buffer; consequently this stress contributes additively, while the genuinely vertical residuals carry a positive power of delta and may pass through finite-stage exponential constants. The theorem is deliberately stated as a reduction theorem. Under the explicitly listed structural inputs--prepared pressure-covariance closure, weak horizontal-defect admissibility, sharp admissible-time trace tightness, singular-stratum tangent-cone inputs, strict limiting smoothing and decay, finite-window trace-cost/Newton solvability, and the vertical-duality active-residual estimate--we derive r_reg(0,0) >= c_{M,theta} |log C_3(1)|^{-sigma/3}. The paper does not constitute an unconditional resolution of the logarithmic one-component regularity problem. Its contribution is a theorem-driven reduction: strict-shadow selection failure is reduced to a finite-mode flat trace obstruction, and that obstruction is eliminated, conditionally, by vertical duality forced by the full three-dimensional vertical momentum equation.

AI.tldr: This paper proves a conditional finite-scale reduction theorem for the local one-component regularity problem of 3D Navier-Stokes equations. It shows that if certain structural conditions hold, a lower bound for the regularity radius can be derived in terms of the critical vertical component and a logarithmic factor. The work reduces the problem to a finite-mode flat trace obstruction that can be conditionally eliminated via vertical duality.
AI.motivation: To make progress on the one-component regularity problem for the 3D Navier-Stokes equations, where only one velocity component (the vertical one) is assumed small, by establishing a conditional reduction theorem that connects this problem to more tractable structural conditions and finite-mode obstructions.
AI.method: The argument uses scale-invariant bounds, compares the solution with a two-and-a-half-dimensional shadow class via harmonic-pressure quotient topology, treats the Reynolds commutator as a positive covariance stress with an unresolved-variance buffer, and relies on multiple structural inputs (pressure-covariance closure, weak horizontal-defect admissibility, sharp admissible-time trace tightness, etc.) to derive the lower bound.
AI.result: Under the listed structural inputs, the regularity radius satisfies r_reg(0,0) >= c_{M,theta} |log C_3(1)|^{-sigma/3}. The paper does not constitute an unconditional resolution but provides a theorem-driven reduction where strict-shadow selection failure reduces to a finite-mode flat trace obstruction.
AI.conclusion: The paper advances the one-component regularity problem by providing a conditional reduction framework. The obstruction to strict-shadow selection is traced to a finite-mode flat trace obstruction, which can be conditionally eliminated by vertical duality from the full 3D vertical momentum equation. The result is not unconditional but provides a structured pathway for future resolution.

title: Neural-Parameterized Cellular Automata for Wildfire Spread
summary: Traditional wildfire models rely on rigid, low-dimensional parameters and static fuel maps, frequently underpredicting fire spread. To address this weakness, we introduce a hybrid deep-learning parameterized Probabilistic Cellular Automata (CA) framework implemented in JAX. Our approach employs a Multi-Scale Convolutional Neural Network to dynamically generate spatially varying parameters that govern fire-spread probability, wind alignment, and slope influence. This hybrid design captures complex, nonlinear environmental interactions while preserving the physical interpretability of the underlying three-state CA. The JAX implementation enables hardware acceleration and gradient-based parameter calibration. Evaluated on six large-scale wildfires in the western United States, the model maintains IoU > 0.6 over 72-hour forecast horizons after a 10-day data assimilation window during which the model is fitted incrementally to observed perimeters; the resulting forecast is a conditional projection of fire growth under the suppression regime already ncoded in those observations.

AI.tldr: A hybrid deep-learning CA model in JAX uses CNNs to dynamically generate fire-spread parameters, achieving IoU > 0.6 over 72-hour forecasts after 10-day assimilation on six large wildfires.
AI.motivation: Traditional wildfire models use rigid parameters and static fuel maps, leading to underprediction of fire spread.
AI.method: A hybrid framework combining Probabilistic Cellular Automata with a Multi-Scale CNN that dynamically generates spatially varying parameters for fire-spread, wind, and slope; implemented in JAX for hardware acceleration and gradient-based calibration.
AI.result: IoU > 0.6 over 72-hour forecast horizons after a 10-day data assimilation window on six western US wildfires.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid model improves forecast accuracy while maintaining physical interpretability; forecasts are conditional projections under the encoded suppression regime.

title: A Decoupled Low-Order Conforming Mixed Finite Element Method for a Three-Dimensional Fourth-Order Singularly Perturbed Problem
summary: This paper develops a decoupled low-order conforming finite element method for a fourth-order elliptic singular perturbation problem in three dimensions. By means of a generalized Helmholtz decomposition, the problem is reduced to two second-order elliptic problems and a system of generalized singularly perturbed Stokes-type equations subject to a curl-free constraint. The former are discretized by standard linear finite elements. For the latter, we employ the MINI element and show that, after adding an $L^2$ term involving a Lagrange multiplier, the resulting discretization becomes robust with respect to the perturbation parameter. We further establish an error estimate of order $h^{1/2}$ uniform with respect to the perturbation parameter. Numerical experiments are included to support the theory.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a decoupled low-order conforming finite element method for a fourth-order elliptic singular perturbation problem in 3D, using Helmholtz decomposition to reduce it to second-order problems and Stokes-type equations, achieving robust h^(1/2) error estimates.
AI.motivation: To solve fourth-order elliptic singular perturbation problems in 3D efficiently with low-order conforming finite elements, addressing challenges in robustness with respect to the perturbation parameter.
AI.method: Uses generalized Helmholtz decomposition to decouple the fourth-order problem into two second-order elliptic problems and a system of singularly perturbed Stokes-type equations with a curl-free constraint. Discretizes second-order problems with standard linear elements and the Stokes-type system with MINI elements, adding an L^2 Lagrange multiplier term for robustness.
AI.result: Achieves a uniform error estimate of order h^(1/2) with respect to the perturbation parameter, validated by numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed decoupled method is robust and effective for fourth-order elliptic singular perturbation problems in 3D, with confirmed theoretical convergence rates.

title: Dirichlet eigenfunctions have non-zero mean for generic domains
summary: Let $D \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ ($d \geq 2$) be a bounded connected open set of class $C^m$ with $m \geq 3$. We prove that, for a generic domain $Ω\in \mathcal{O}^m(D)$ in the sense of Baire category for the Micheletti topology, every Dirichlet-Laplacian eigenfunction has nonzero mean, $\int_Ω\varphi_n \neq 0$ for all $n \geq 1$. This answers a question raised by Steinerberger and Venkatraman. The proof rests on a shape-derivative computation whose degeneracy is identified with an overdetermined elliptic problem of Schiffer type. Rather than resolving this problem, we bypass it through a Baire-category argument based on the meagerness of domains with analytic boundary. As a consequence, we obtain that for a generic domain the Dirichlet heat equation is approximately controllable, and rapidly stabilizable, i.e. stabilizable at any prescribed exponential decay rate, by a single spatially homogeneous scalar control, via the Fattorini-Hautus criterion.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that for most (generic) domains, every Dirichlet-Laplacian eigenfunction has nonzero mean, using a Baire-category argument to bypass a difficult Schiffer-type problem. This implies generic domains are approximately controllable and rapidly stabilizable with a single homogeneous scalar control.
AI.motivation: To answer a question raised by Steinerberger and Venkatraman about whether there exist domains where all Dirichlet eigenfunctions have nonzero mean, and to establish generic controllability/stabilizability properties for the Dirichlet heat equation.
AI.method: The proof uses shape-derivative computation, identifies degeneracy with an overdetermined elliptic problem of Schiffer type, and bypasses resolving it via a Baire-category argument exploiting the meagerness (topological smallness) of domains with analytic boundary in the Micheletti topology.
AI.result: For a generic domain (in the Baire sense with Micheletti topology), every Dirichlet-Laplacian eigenfunction has nonzero mean. Consequently, the Dirichlet heat equation on such domains is approximately controllable and rapidly stabilizable by a single spatially homogeneous scalar control.
AI.conclusion: Generic domains enjoy strong controllability and stabilizability properties for the heat equation, bypassing the need to fully resolve the Schiffer-type overdetermined problem. The approach demonstrates the power of generic analysis using topological arguments.

title: Universal Information-Theoretic Structure of the Quasi-Stationary Domany--Kinzel Automaton
summary: We characterize the quasi-stationary distribution (QSD) of the bond directed-percolation line of the Domany--Kinzel automaton using a matrix-product-state representation of the probability distribution, obtained by projecting out the absorbing state and iterating the transfer matrix. Unlike moment- or sampling-based methods, this yields the full conditional distribution and direct access to information-theoretic diagnostics. The spatial structure of the QSD changes sharply across the transition: the active phase is bulk-like with finite density, whereas in the inactive phase the surviving activity collapses into a single flock occupying a vanishing fraction of the chain, with an internal filling that ranges from a single cluster deep in the inactive phase to a loose, partially filled group near criticality. This picture carries a sharp information-theoretic signature: throughout the inactive phase the bipartite mutual information of the QSD equals the entropy of a single binary choice -- whether the flock lies to the left or right of the cut -- so the surviving clusters together encode just one bit of positional information, corresponding to a single effective cluster. The approach extends matrix-product-state techniques to the projected eigenvector defining a QSD, opening information-theoretic diagnostics for absorbing-state systems that bulk-observable methods cannot reach.

AI.tldr: The paper uses matrix-product-state representations to analyze the quasi-stationary distribution of the Domany-Kinzel automaton, revealing that the inactive phase collapses into a single effective flock encoding just one bit of positional information.
AI.motivation: To characterize the quasi-stationary distribution (QSD) of the bond directed-percolation line of the Domany-Kinzel automaton using a matrix-product-state representation, obtaining the full conditional distribution and direct access to information-theoretic diagnostics beyond moment- or sampling-based methods.
AI.method: Matrix-product-state representation of the probability distribution, obtained by projecting out the absorbing state and iterating the transfer matrix.
AI.result: The active phase is bulk-like with finite density, while in the inactive phase surviving activity collapses into a single flock occupying a vanishing fraction of the chain with varying internal filling. The bipartite mutual information equals the entropy of a single binary choice (flock left or right of cut), encoding just one bit of positional information.
AI.conclusion: The approach extends matrix-product-state techniques to the projected eigenvector defining a QSD, enabling information-theoretic diagnostics for absorbing-state systems that bulk-observable methods cannot reach.

title: Symmetric structure-preserving discretization of N-phase incompressible fluid mixtures with arbitrary density ratios
summary:
Diffuse-interface models are a widely used framework for interfacial dynamics in complex fluids, in which interfaces are represented through smooth transition layers and capillary effects are encoded by a free-energy functional. For incompressible mixtures with more than two phases, however, robust computation is substantially more difficult because the numerical method should preserve the balance structure of the continuum model, maintain the saturation constraint, dissipate energy, and treat all phases symmetrically even when density ratios are arbitrary. Existing structure-preserving methods are largely developed for binary flows or for formulations that distinguish a reference phase, so a genuinely symmetric N-phase discretization remains lacking. The practical problem is therefore to construct a fully-discrete method for N-phase incompressible Navier--Stokes--Cahn--Hilliard mixture models that retains the key thermodynamic and conservation properties of the continuum equations for arbitrary density ratios.
  Here we propose a symmetric fully-discrete method for the N-phase incompressible Navier--Stokes--Cahn--Hilliard mixture model with arbitrary density ratios. The method yields a fully-discrete problem in which every solution satisfies exact phase volume conservation, phase mass conservation, total volume conservation, total mass conservation, and a discrete energy-dissipation law. In addition, if the volume-saturation constraint holds for the initial data, then it is preserved at every time step. We numerically verify these structure-preserving properties and demonstrate the robustness of the method in representative multiphase flow problems. The resulting scheme provides a computational framework for incompressible N-phase mixture flows with complex interfacial dynamics and arbitrary density contrasts.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a symmetric fully-discrete numerical method for N-phase incompressible Navier-Stokes-Cahn-Hilliard mixture models that preserves key thermodynamic and conservation properties (phase volume, phase mass, total volume, total mass, energy dissipation, and saturation constraint) for arbitrary density ratios, verified through numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: Existing structure-preserving methods are largely limited to binary flows or formulations distinguishing a reference phase; a genuinely symmetric N-phase discretization that handles arbitrary density ratios while preserving balance structure, saturation constraint, energy dissipation, and phase symmetry has been lacking.
AI.method: A symmetric fully-discrete method for the N-phase incompressible Navier-Stokes-Cahn-Hilliard mixture model that exactly satisfies phase volume conservation, phase mass conservation, total volume conservation, total mass conservation, a discrete energy-dissipation law, and preserves the volume-saturation constraint at every time step for arbitrary density ratios.
AI.result: Numerical verification confirms structure-preserving properties (exact conservation laws and energy dissipation) and demonstrates robustness in representative multiphase flow problems with complex interfacial dynamics and arbitrary density contrasts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scheme provides a computational framework for incompressible N-phase mixture flows with complex interfacial dynamics and arbitrary density contrasts, filling the gap for a genuinely symmetric N-phase structure-preserving discretization.

title: Local and global strong solutions to a reduced model for inviscid micropolar fluids
summary:
This paper investigates the well-posedness issue for a reduced two-dimensional model of micropolar fluids. This reduced model presents a coupling between an Euler-type equation for the velocity field of the fluid and a transport-diffusion equation for the microrotation field (which is a scalar field, in this setting).
  We establish the local existence and uniqueness of strong solutions in the scale of Besov space $B^s_{p,1}$ having regularity index $s\geq1+2/p$. Furthermore, in the subcritical case when $s>1+2/p$, we prove that these solutions exist globally in time. The global persistence of regularity in the critical setting $s=1+2/p$ remains open.

AI.tldr: This paper proves local and global existence/uniqueness of strong solutions for a reduced 2D micropolar fluid model in Besov spaces, with a critical regularity gap still open.
AI.motivation: To establish well-posedness (existence and uniqueness) for a reduced 2D micropolar fluid model, which couples an Euler-type velocity equation with a transport-diffusion equation for microrotation.
AI.method: The analysis is conducted in the scale of Besov spaces \(B^s_{p,1}\) with regularity index \(s \geq 1 + 2/p\). The authors study both local and global well-posedness, distinguishing the critical case \(s = 1 + 2/p\) from the subcritical case \(s > 1 + 2/p\).
AI.result: Local existence and uniqueness is established for \(s \geq 1 + 2/p\). Global existence is proved for the subcritical case \(s > 1 + 2/p\). The critical case \(s = 1 + 2/p\) remains open for global persistence.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous well-posedness framework for the reduced 2D micropolar fluid model in Besov spaces, leaving the global regularity at the critical regularity threshold as an open problem.

title: HAMNO: A Hierarchical Adaptive Multi-scale Neural Operator with Physics-Informed Learning for Dynamical Systems
summary:
Neural operators provide a powerful framework for learning solution mappings of partial differential equations directly in function space. However, many existing architectures still struggle to represent nonlinear time-dependent systems that involve multi-scale structures, long-range interactions, and stable long-time evolution. In this work, we introduce the Hierarchical Adaptive Multi-scale Neural Operator (HAMNO), a neural-operator architecture that combines local convolutional representations, global spectral operators, and hierarchical encoder-decoder processing. The central component of HAMNO is a data-dependent gating mechanism that adaptively balances local and global information at each spatial location, allowing the model to resolve fine-scale features while preserving long-range dependencies.
  We further develop a physics-informed extension, PI-HAMNO, based on a multi-objective loss strategy that combines data fitting with strong- and weak-form physics constraints. The strong-form term penalizes the domain-integrated squared PDE residual in physical coordinates, while the weak-form term is constructed by multiplying the governing residual by finite-element test functions and evaluating the resulting element integrals using centroid-based tetrahedral quadrature. The framework is evaluated on non-periodic Allen-Cahn (AC), Cahn-Hilliard (CH), and Swift-Hohenberg (SH) equations defined on cubic domains. Across long-horizon rollout, data-limited training, out-of-distribution initial-condition shifts, and random-seed variations, HAMNO improves predictive accuracy over standard neural-operator baselines, while PI-HAMNO further enhances stability, physical consistency, and data efficiency. The implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/MBamdad/HAMNO .

AI.tldr: HAMNO is a neural operator architecture that combines local convolutions, global spectral operators, and hierarchical encoding with an adaptive gating mechanism to handle multi-scale, long-range PDE dynamics. PI-HAMNO extends it with physics-informed multi-objective loss (strong + weak form constraints). Both outperform baselines on Allen-Cahn, Cahn-Hilliard, and Swift-Hohenberg equations.
AI.motivation: Existing neural operators struggle with nonlinear time-dependent PDEs involving multi-scale structures, long-range interactions, and stable long-time evolution.
AI.method: HAMNO combines local convolutional representations, global spectral operators, hierarchical encoder-decoder processing, and a data-dependent gating mechanism to adaptively balance local and global info. PI-HAMNO adds a multi-objective loss with strong-form (squared PDE residual) and weak-form (finite-element test functions with tetrahedral quadrature) physics constraints.
AI.result: On non-periodic Allen-Cahn, Cahn-Hilliard, and Swift-Hohenberg equations, HAMNO improves accuracy over standard neural-operator baselines in long-horizon rollout, data-limited training, out-of-distribution shifts, and random-seed variations. PI-HAMNO further improves stability, physical consistency, and data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HAMNO and PI-HAMNO effectively address multi-scale and long-range challenges in neural operators, achieving better accuracy, stability, and data efficiency for nonlinear time-dependent PDEs.

title: An improvement on B-spline basis condition number
summary: In this short note, we improve the upper bound on the condition number of the univariate B-spline basis of order $k$ from $k 2^k$ to $O(\sqrt{k}\log k\,2^k)$ for all $k\ge 2$.

AI.tldr: This paper improves the upper bound on the condition number of the univariate B-spline basis of order k, reducing it from k·2^k to O(√k log k · 2^k) for all k ≥ 2.
AI.motivation: To tighten the known theoretical bound on the condition number of B-spline bases, which is important for numerical stability in computational geometry and approximation theory.
AI.method: The author provides a refined analytical derivation that yields a tighter upper bound on the condition number using more careful combinatorial or asymptotic estimates.
AI.result: The new upper bound is O(√k log k · 2^k), improving upon the previous k·2^k bound.
AI.conclusion: The condition number of the univariate B-spline basis grows more slowly than previously thought, suggesting better numerical stability for higher-order B-splines.

title: Some results for a stationary Navier-Stokes equation with a rough drift in a weighted functional framework
summary: In this article, we study some classes of solutions for a stationary Navier-Stokes equation where we consider a rough drift given by a singular integral operator which does not belong to the classical Calder{ó}n-Zygmund family of singular integral operators. Given a small external force, we will construct solutions to this system in the framework of weighted Morrey-Sobolev spaces. The use of Morrey-based Sobolev spaces provides a more general setting than the usual Lebesgue-based Sobolev spaces, and the presence of Muckenhoupt weights will allow us to present some existence and uniqueness results from several points of view.

AI.tldr: This paper studies stationary Navier-Stokes equations with rough drift (singular integral operators outside Calderón-Zygmund class). Using weighted Morrey-Sobolev spaces with Muckenhoupt weights, the authors construct solutions for small external forces and prove existence/uniqueness results in this broader functional framework.
AI.motivation: To extend the study of Navier-Stokes equations to cases involving rough drifts (singular integral operators beyond the classical Calderón-Zygmund family), which requires more general function spaces than standard Lebesgue-Sobolev spaces.
AI.method: The analysis is conducted in weighted Morrey-Sobolev spaces, incorporating Muckenhoupt weights. This framework generalizes classical Lebesgue-based Sobolev spaces and allows handling of the rough singular integral drift operator.
AI.result: Existence and uniqueness of solutions are established for stationary Navier-Stokes equations with rough drift, given a small external force, within the weighted Morrey-Sobolev space setting.
AI.conclusion: Weighted Morrey-Sobolev spaces provide a suitable and more general framework for solving Navier-Stokes equations with non-standard singular integral drifts, offering multiple perspectives on existence and uniqueness.

title: Multilayer Screening of Double and Conventional Perovskite Solar Cells Using SCAPS-1D and Machine Learning: Optimization of ETL, HTL, and Absorber for High-Efficiency Architectures
summary: The combinatorial design space of multilayer perovskite solar cells is vast, yet exhaustive experimental or computational screening of all possible material combinations remains impractical. Here, we integrate SCAPS-1D device simulations with machine learning to systematically explore 125 device architectures constructed from five electron transport layers (ETL), five absorbers (including lead-free double perovskites), and five hole transport layers (HTL). A representative subset of configurations is used to train a machine learning (ML) model, which predicts the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the remaining unexplored structures. Leave-One-Group-Out cross-validation yields a Spearman rank correlation, demonstrating reliable ranking capability. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) analysis reveals that the HTL band gap, absorber band gap, and ETL electron affinity are the most influential descriptors, providing physical insights into interfacial recombination and charge extraction. The machine learning model identifies several high-performance configurations that are subsequently verified by full SCAPS-1D simulations. Among them, the device FTO/TiO$_2$/Cs$_2$AgBiBr$_6$/NiO/Ag achieves a PCE of 28.85%, and the ML-suggested structure FTO/SnO$_2$/Cs$_2$AgInBr$_6$/NiO/Ag exhibits 28.62%, outperforming a closely related literature architecture by approximately 4% absolute. Notably, eight of the top-11 structures employ the lead-free double perovskite Cs$_2$AgInBr$_6$. This work demonstrates that a physics-based, data-driven workflow combining SCAPS-1D, ML, and SHAP can accelerate the discovery of high-efficiency, environmentally friendly perovskite solar cells while providing transparent design rules. The approach is generalizable to other multilayer optoelectronic systems.

AI.tldr: This paper combines SCAPS-1D simulations with machine learning and SHAP analysis to screen 125 perovskite solar cell architectures, identifying high-performance lead-free devices including FTO/TiO₂/Cs₂AgBiBr₆/NiO/Ag (28.85% PCE) and FTO/SnO₂/Cs₂AgInBr₆/NiO/Ag (28.62% PCE).
AI.motivation: The combinatorial design space of multilayer perovskite solar cells is vast, making exhaustive experimental or computational screening of all possible material combinations impractical.
AI.method: Integrated SCAPS-1D device simulations with machine learning (ML) and SHAP analysis to systematically explore 125 device architectures (5 ETLs × 5 absorbers × 5 HTLs). Used representative subset to train ML model predicting PCE, with Leave-One-Group-Out cross-validation and SHAP for feature importance.
AI.result: Top architectures: FTO/TiO₂/Cs₂AgBiBr₆/NiO/Ag (28.85% PCE) and FTO/SnO₂/Cs₂AgInBr₆/NiO/Ag (28.62% PCE). Eight of top-11 structures use lead-free Cs₂AgInBr₆. ML-suggested structure outperforms literature architecture by ~4% absolute.
AI.conclusion: A physics-based, data-driven workflow combining SCAPS-1D, ML, and SHAP can accelerate discovery of high-efficiency, environmentally friendly perovskite solar cells while providing transparent design rules. Approach is generalizable to other multilayer optoelectronic systems.

title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
summary: Inverse problems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs) are central to computational mechanics and are commonly solved by adjoint-based optimization, while physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have emerged as a flexible alternative. Their relative performance remains difficult to assess because the two approaches are often compared under different formulations, parameterizations, optimizers, and regularization choices. We present a fair comparison of adjoint optimization and PINNs for PDE-constrained inverse problems. From a common abstract formulation, we instantiate both methods on identical domains, governing equations, observation models, and regularization terms, while matching the optimizer, unknown parameterization, and arithmetic precision wherever applicable. The benchmarks include unsteady Burgers, noisy Darcy permeability inversion, three-dimensional Allen--Cahn reaction identification, and unsteady Navier--Stokes viscosity identification. The results show that the representation of the unknown largely determines the preferred method: grid-based fields favor the discrete adjoint, whereas neural representations are native to PINNs and relevant for closure and constitutive modeling. For time-dependent problems, adjoint inversion can be dominated by trajectory storage and differentiation, while PINNs provide satisfactory reconstructions at lower cost. A PINN-warm-started adjoint strategy then recovers adjoint-level accuracy at substantially reduced cost.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a fair, apples-to-apples comparison of adjoint-based optimization and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving PDE-constrained inverse problems, finding that grid-based representations favor adjoint methods while neural representations favor PINNs, and that PINNs can be more efficient for time-dependent problems, with a hybrid warm-start strategy offering the best of both worlds.
AI.motivation: To resolve the difficulty in assessing relative performance of adjoint optimization and PINNs for PDE-constrained inverse problems, since prior comparisons often used different formulations, parameterizations, optimizers, and regularization choices.
AI.method: Both methods are instantiated from a common abstract formulation on identical domains, governing equations, observation models, and regularization terms, with matched optimizer, unknown parameterization, and arithmetic precision. Benchmarks include: unsteady Burgers, noisy Darcy permeability inversion, 3D Allen-Cahn reaction identification, and unsteady Navier-Stokes viscosity identification.
AI.result: Grid-based unknown fields favor the discrete adjoint method; neural representations are native to PINNs. For time-dependent problems, adjoint inversion suffers from high trajectory storage/differentiation costs, while PINNs achieve satisfactory reconstructions at lower cost. A PINN-warm-started adjoint strategy recovers adjoint-level accuracy at substantially reduced cost.
AI.conclusion: The best method depends on the representation of the unknown parameter. A hybrid approach (PINN warm-start + adjoint refinement) can combine efficiency and accuracy effectively.

title: Mean-field imitation dynamics on fast assortative networks
summary: The emergence of cooperation in structured populations is fundamental to the success of human societies. Physical and online networks can drive behavioural change by altering who people interact with, thereby modifying social pressures. In this paper, we study imitation dynamics in a population of self-interested agents playing a continuous strategy Prisoner's Dilemma on a dynamically evolving weighted network. In the fast-network regime, we incorporate the edge weights into the strategy evolution before deriving and analysing the large population mean-field limit. Without noise, we establish well-posedness and show the solution collapses to a single Dirac mass. For initially separated clusters, we identify a payoff threshold and sufficient conditions for the overall level of cooperation to increase. We then introduce stochastic strategy updates, and obtain a non-local Fokker-Planck equation in the mean-field limit. We rigorously prove existence and uniqueness of stationary distributions, and show linear stability under sufficient noise. Numerics illustrate that noise can transform the deterministic consensus into stable cooperative stationary behaviour. These findings show that the fast adaptive interactions and stochastic exploration can jointly support the emergence of stable cooperation at a population level.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how fast-evolving weighted networks and stochastic strategy updates promote stable cooperation in the Prisoner's Dilemma, using mean-field limits and Fokker-Planck equations to show noise can transform deterministic consensus into stable cooperative behavior.
AI.motivation: To understand how structured populations and dynamic interaction networks (physical/online) drive cooperation emergence, specifically by modeling imitation dynamics with weighted edges and stochastic effects.
AI.method: Analyzes continuous-strategy Prisoner's Dilemma on dynamically evolving weighted networks in the fast-network regime; derives mean-field limit (Dirac mass collapse without noise, non-local Fokker-Planck equation with noise); proves well-posedness, existence/uniqueness of stationary distributions, and linear stability.
AI.result: Without noise, solution collapses to a single Dirac mass; with identified payoff thresholds, cooperation can increase. With noise, stationary distributions exist uniquely and are linearly stable under sufficient noise. Numerics show noise transforms deterministic consensus into stable cooperative states.
AI.conclusion: Fast adaptive interactions and stochastic exploration jointly support the emergence of stable cooperation at the population level.

title: Effects of microstructural heterogeneity on the macroscopic spectrum of elastically accommodated grain-boundary sliding
comment: Submitted to Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
summary: Elastically accommodated grain-boundary sliding (EAGBS) is a plausible source of upper-mantle seismic attenuation and dispersion, yet classical theory predicts a localized Debye-like peak that is absent or only weakly expressed in dry olivine experiments. Here we test whether microstructural heterogeneity can explain this discrepancy using 2-D finite-element simulations on periodic Voronoi tessellations. We find that irregular grain geometry changes the baseline EAGBS response relative to the regular hexagonal benchmark, but increasing grain-size variance alone produces only modest changes in modulus and peak height, with little spectral broadening. In contrast, a broad distribution of grain-boundary viscosities progressively suppresses and broadens the Debye-like loss peak into a weak background spanning a wide frequency interval. This broadening arises from the superposition of many localized relaxation processes with distinct characteristic timescales and motivates a reduced-order 0-D description of the aggregate response. These results suggest that the absence of a pronounced EAGBS peak in dry olivine does not necessarily imply the absence of EAGBS mechanism itself. If grain boundaries sample a sufficiently broad viscosity distribution, the macroscopic EAGBS contribution may appear experimentally only as part of a broad attenuation background, while still remaining relevant for upper-mantle seismic attenuation and velocity dispersion.

AI.tldr: Using 2D simulations on Voronoi tessellations, this paper shows that a broad distribution of grain-boundary viscosities—not irregular grain geometry—can suppress and broaden the Debye-like EAGBS peak into a weak background, explaining why dry olivine experiments lack a pronounced peak while the mechanism may still operate in the upper mantle.
AI.motivation: Classical EAGBS theory predicts a Debye-like attenuation peak that is absent or weakly expressed in dry olivine experiments; the authors test whether microstructural heterogeneity (irregular grain geometry vs. viscosity distribution) can resolve this discrepancy.
AI.method: 2-D finite-element simulations on periodic Voronoi tessellations, comparing regular hexagonal benchmarks against irregular grain geometries and varying grain-boundary viscosity distributions.
AI.result: Irregular grain geometry alone produces modest changes; a broad distribution of grain-boundary viscosities progressively suppresses and broadens the Debye peak into a weak background spanning a wide frequency interval, due to superposition of localized relaxation processes.
AI.conclusion: The absence of a pronounced EAGBS peak in dry olivine does not rule out the EAGBS mechanism; a sufficiently broad viscosity distribution could make the contribution appear as part of a broad attenuation background, remaining relevant for upper-mantle seismic attenuation.

title: MATLAB-Based Layerwise Self-Adaptive Physics-Informed Neural Network in Applications to Multidimensional Coupled Burgers' Equations with High Reynolds Numbers
summary:
This paper presents an improved physics-informed neural network for simulating the spatio-temporal solution profile of the multidimensional coupled Burgers' equations with high Reynolds numbers. As time evolves, the sharp shock fronts emerge in the solution, creating significant computational challenges for the conventional mesh-based numerical methods. In particular, numerical methods such as finite differences and finite elements suffer from poor stability and strong mesh dependency when resolving the steep solution gradients. To address these challenges, the proposed framework employs a layerwise self-adaptive weighting strategy that dynamically adjusts the penalty weights for the physics residual, initial conditions, and boundary conditions throughout training. Moreover, the framework uses a dual-phase optimization strategy to achieve more stable convergence.
  To check the effectiveness and accuracy of the proposed framework, a set of numerical experiments is conducted to compare it with the standard Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) with and without Limited-memory Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (L-BFGS) optimization. Numerical results exhibit that the proposed framework achieves higher accuracy in terms of relative $L_2-$ error norm than the standard PINN and is able to capture the development of sharp shock fronts as time evolves in the solution.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an improved physics-informed neural network with layerwise self-adaptive weighting and dual-phase optimization to simulate the multidimensional coupled Burgers' equations at high Reynolds numbers, achieving higher accuracy and better capture of sharp shock fronts compared to standard PINN.
AI.motivation: Conventional mesh-based numerical methods (finite differences, finite elements) suffer from poor stability and strong mesh dependency when solving multidimensional coupled Burgers' equations with high Reynolds numbers, especially as sharp shock fronts emerge over time.
AI.method: The proposed framework introduces (1) a layerwise self-adaptive weighting strategy that dynamically adjusts penalty weights for physics residual, initial conditions, and boundary conditions during training, and (2) a dual-phase optimization strategy for more stable convergence.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show the proposed framework achieves higher accuracy in relative L2-error norm compared to standard PINN (with and without L-BFGS optimization) and successfully captures the sharp shock front development over time.
AI.conclusion: The improved PINN framework effectively addresses the challenges of simulating high-Reynolds-number Burgers' equations with sharp shock fronts, outperforming standard PINN methods in both accuracy and stability.

title: Trace and Hardy-Sobolev type inequalities and applications to a quasilinear elliptic problem in half-space
summary:
In the present paper we are dealing with the following quasilinear elliptic problem:
  \begin{equation*}
  \begin{cases}
  -\mathrm{div}(ρ(x_N) |\nabla u|^{p-2}\nabla u) =a|u|^{s-2}u &\mbox{in }&\ \mathbb{R}^N_+,
  -|\nabla u|^{p-2}\frac{\partial u}{\partial x_N}=b|u|^{q-2}u&\mbox{on }&\
  \mathbb{R}^{N-1},
  \end{cases}\ \end{equation*} where $a,b\in \mathbb{R}$, $p,q,s\in(1,\infty)$ and $ρ$ is a continuous positive function on $[0,+\infty)$. We first prove new and sharp embedding results that we establish for the associted weighted energy spaces. In application, we establish existence and regularity of weak solutions to the above problem. We also prove for this problem the nonexistence of nontrivial weak solutions by a new Pohozaev-type identity we obtain. The new results about existence and nonexistence highlight the role of the weight $ρ$ on the solvability of the problem contrasting strongly with those when $ρ$ is constant.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a quasilinear elliptic problem with a weight function ρ, proving new sharp embedding results, existence/regularity of weak solutions, and a nonexistence result via a new Pohozaev-type identity, highlighting how the weight ρ affects solvability compared to the constant-weight case.
AI.motivation: To understand how a non-constant weight function ρ in a quasilinear elliptic problem affects solvability, existence, regularity, and nonexistence of weak solutions, contrasting with the well-studied constant-weight case.
AI.method: The authors first prove new sharp embedding results for the associated weighted energy spaces. Then they establish existence and regularity of weak solutions. Finally, they derive a new Pohozaev-type identity to prove nonexistence of nontrivial weak solutions.
AI.result: Sharp embedding results for weighted energy spaces; existence and regularity of weak solutions; nonexistence of nontrivial weak solutions via a new Pohozaev-type identity; demonstration that the weight ρ plays a crucial role in solvability, contrasting strongly with the constant-ρ case.
AI.conclusion: The weight ρ fundamentally influences the solvability of the problem, leading to qualitatively different existence/nonexistence results compared to when ρ is constant.

title: Numerical simulations of the spread from the mean of the SLE and Multiple SLE dynamics
comment: Note that an updated version of this paper is officially published in the Journal Research in Statistics (2026 Vol 4 Issue 1) that has more updated experiments and discussions. That version is also open access under the Creative Commons Attribution License. It is availabe at https://doi.org/10.1080/27684520.2026.2648957
summary: The Schramm-Loewner Evolution (SLE) describes a family of fractal curves that arise in the study of the scaling limits of many planar Statistical Physics models. These curves are modeled using the Loewner Differential Equation for the conformal maps $g_t(z)$ with a Brownian motion driver. Using Euler's Method, in the current work we performed numerical experiments to study at a fixed time the quantities $|g_t(z) - \overline{g_t(z)}|$ and $Re(g_t(z)) - Re(\overline{g_t(z)})$, where $Re$ denotes the real part and $\overline{g_t(z)}$ refers to the sample average. These random variables measure the 'spread' of the dynamics from the average behavior at fixed time. One of the scopes of this work is to give numerical predictions for future theoretical investigations on these quantities. When investigating these quantities in the SLE case our experiments predict that the distribution is bimodal when the dynamics started close to the origin, and it can become bell-shaped if the dynamics is started further from the origin. In the second part, we performed experiments for a Multiple SLE model whose driver is Dyson Brownian Motion. Due to singularity in the dynamics of the drivers and the many data points needed, this part is challenging from a computational perspective. In the multiple SLE case, our experiments predict that the distribution is bell-shaped in all cases. In addition, we check the changes in the distributions as we vary the parameter $κ$ in the SLE case and $β$ in the Multiple SLE case.

AI.tldr: This paper numerically investigates the spread of dynamics in SLE and Multiple SLE curves using Euler's Method, finding bimodal distributions near the origin for SLE (turning bell-shaped further away) and bell-shaped distributions for Multiple SLE across all cases.
AI.motivation: To provide numerical predictions for future theoretical investigations on the spread of SLE dynamics from average behavior, specifically studying quantities |g_t(z) - sample average| and Re(g_t(z)) - Re(sample average).
AI.method: Euler's Method applied to the Loewner Differential Equation; numerical experiments on SLE (Brownian motion driver) and Multiple SLE (Dyson Brownian Motion driver); analysis of distribution shapes (bimodal vs. bell-shaped) as functions of initial position and parameters κ and β.
AI.result: SLE: distribution is bimodal when started near the origin, becomes bell-shaped when started further away. Multiple SLE: distribution is bell-shaped in all cases. Distribution shapes vary with parameter κ (SLE) and β (Multiple SLE).
AI.conclusion: The numerical predictions offer guidance for future theoretical work on these spread quantities in SLE and Multiple SLE, with clear transitions in distribution shape depending on initial conditions and model parameters.

title: Hartee-type heat equation associated to fractional anharmonic oscillator on weighted modulation spaces
summary: We study Hartree-type nonlinear heat equations associated with fractional generalised anharmonic oscillators \(A_{k,l}\) in weighted modulation spaces. We first derive Strichartz-type estimates for the associated heat semigroup and then apply them to establish global well-posedness for small initial data. For \(s>\frac{d}{q'}\), the result is obtained via trilinear estimates exploiting the algebra property of the weighted modulation space, \(M^{p,q}_s\). We further establish refined trilinear estimates that bypass the algebra structure, thereby extending the global well-posedness theory to the wider range \(s\ge 0\).

AI.tldr: The paper studies Hartree-type nonlinear heat equations with fractional generalised anharmonic oscillators in weighted modulation spaces, deriving Strichartz estimates and establishing global well-posedness for small initial data under varying smoothness conditions.
AI.motivation: To extend global well-posedness theory for Hartree-type nonlinear heat equations with fractional generalised anharmonic oscillators to broader regularity ranges in weighted modulation spaces.
AI.method: First derives Strichartz-type estimates for the associated heat semigroup, then applies them with trilinear estimates exploiting the algebra property of weighted modulation spaces (for s > d/q'), and refines these to bypass the algebra structure (for s ≥ 0).
AI.result: Global well-posedness for small initial data is established first for s > d/q' using algebra property, then extended to s ≥ 0 via refined trilinear estimates.
AI.conclusion: The refined trilinear estimates successfully relax the smoothness requirement, proving global well-posedness for a wider range of regularity exponents.

title: A coupled finite element formulation for chemo-mechano-thermodynamical contact and its application to bonding and debonding
summary: This work presents a finite element formulation for coupled chemo-mechano-thermodynamical large deformation contact. The formulation is based on the contact theory of Sauer et al. (2022) that contains six coupled (but separate) fields: the deformation and temperature of the two contacting bodies, as well as an interfacial bonding field and interfacial temperature. The latter is governed by the chemical and mechanical energy dissipation at the interface. Here the focus is placed on the evolution of bonding and debonding, and how it is coupled to the mechanical and thermal contact state. Several elementary models are proposed for this based on a quadratic contact potential. The resulting contact formulation becomes very general and versatile, which is illustrated by several challenging examples. They include pressure- and gap- depended bonding, exothermic bonding reactions, thermal hardening and thermal expansion, as well as simultaneous bonding and debonding. They are based on a monolithic finite element implementation using classical and isogeometric shape functions together with implicit time integration. Its full linearization, required for the Newton-Raphson solution method, is also provided. If bonding sites are material points, the bonding variable can be condensed-out locally.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a finite element formulation for coupled chemo-mechano-thermodynamical contact with large deformations, focusing on bonding/debonding evolution coupled to mechanical and thermal states using quadratic contact potentials and monolithic FE implementation.
AI.motivation: To develop a general and versatile contact formulation that captures the complex coupling between chemical bonding/debonding, mechanical deformation, and thermal effects in large deformation contact problems.
AI.method: Extends Sauer et al.'s (2022) contact theory with six coupled fields; proposes elementary bonding/debonding models based on quadratic contact potential; uses monolithic FE implementation with classical/isogeometric shape functions and implicit time integration; provides full linearization for Newton-Raphson solution.
AI.result: Demonstrates the formulation's versatility through examples: pressure/gap-dependent bonding, exothermic bonding reactions, thermal hardening, thermal expansion, and simultaneous bonding/debonding.
AI.conclusion: The proposed formulation enables local condensation of the bonding variable when bonding sites are material points, making it computationally efficient while capturing complex chemo-mechano-thermodynamical coupling in contact mechanics.

title: Geometric bias in eigenspace perturbation under random heterogeneous noise
summary: Spectral methods rely fundamentally on the stability of principal eigenspaces under random perturbations. Classically, this stability is quantified by the Davis-Kahan and Wedin theorems, which bound the eigenspace error using the operator norm of the noise and the relevant spectral gaps. While these worst-case bounds are sharp for arbitrary deterministic perturbations, they can be wasteful in the low-rank signal-plus-random-noise setting, as they fail to capture the fine-grained interaction between the signal geometry and the noise distribution. In this paper, we study the spectral perturbation of signal-plus-noise matrices corrupted by sparse, random noise with an arbitrary, inhomogeneous variance profile. We demonstrate that under heterogeneous noise variances, the empirical eigenvectors suffer a systematic, deterministic geometric bias that is entirely invisible to classical perturbation bounds. By leveraging the Quadratic Vector Equation (QVE) and establishing fine-grained isotropic local laws, we derive near-optimal, non-asymptotic perturbation bounds for the leading eigenspaces in the operator and $2\to\infty$ norms. The bounds separate the usual signal-to-noise contribution, stochastic fluctuations, and structured geometric bias terms determined by the alignment between the signal eigenspaces and the row-wise variance profile.

AI.tldr: This paper studies spectral perturbation of signal-plus-noise matrices with heterogeneous, sparse noise. It reveals that under inhomogeneous variance profiles, empirical eigenvectors exhibit a systematic geometric bias invisible to classical bounds like Davis-Kahan. Using the Quadratic Vector Equation and isotropic local laws, it derives near-optimal non-asymptotic bounds for leading eigenspaces, separating signal-to-noise contributions, stochastic fluctuations, and structured bias from signal-noise alignment.
AI.motivation: Classical spectral perturbation bounds (Davis-Kahan/Wedin) are worst-case and fail to capture fine-grained interactions between signal geometry and noise distribution in low-rank signal-plus-random-noise settings, particularly under heterogeneous noise variance.
AI.method: The paper leverages the Quadratic Vector Equation (QVE) and establishes fine-grained isotropic local laws to derive near-optimal, non-asymptotic perturbation bounds for leading eigenspaces in operator and 2→∞ norms.
AI.result: The bounds separate three components: (1) the usual signal-to-noise contribution, (2) stochastic fluctuations, and (3) a structured geometric bias term determined by alignment between signal eigenspaces and the row-wise variance profile.
AI.conclusion: Classical worst-case bounds are insufficient for heterogeneous noise settings; the derived bounds reveal a systematic geometric bias that must be accounted for, and the QVE-based approach provides near-optimal guarantees.

title: Complex-ellipticity, dimensional estimates and plane wave rigidity in $BV^{\mathbb A}$
summary: We characterize complex-elliptic operators $\mathbb A(D)$ through a hierarchy of overdeterminacy ($\ell$-vanishing) quantifying the structural twisting of their symbols. This framework yields the optimal dimensional estimate for $BV^{\mathbb A}$-functions: a measure ${\mathbb A} u$ cannot concentrate on sets of dimension below $n-1$. Consequently, the jump part of ${\mathbb A} u$ is characterized as an $(n-1)$-dimensional surface measure with density given by the symbol and the two-sided traces. Building on this dimensional bound, we prove that measures satisfying $\frac{{\mathbb A} u}{|{\mathbb A} u|} \in \mathrm{span}\{P_0\}$ precisely decompose into finite sums of one-dimensional $BV$ profiles. Ultimately, these results reveal that complex-ellipticity strictly enforces a plane-wave structure on tangent measures.

AI.tldr: The paper characterizes complex-elliptic operators through overdeterminacy hierarchies, establishes optimal dimensional estimates for BV-functions, and shows complex-ellipticity enforces a plane-wave structure on tangent measures.
AI.motivation: To understand how structural twisting of complex-elliptic operator symbols affects measure concentration and dimensional estimates for BV-functions.
AI.method: Introduces an ℓ-vanishing hierarchy to quantify operator symbol twisting; uses this to derive dimensional bounds and decomposition results.
AI.result: Optimal dimensional estimate: A-measures cannot concentrate on sets of dimension below n-1; jump part characterized as (n-1)-dimensional surface measure; measures satisfying a certain condition decompose into finite sums of 1D BV profiles.
AI.conclusion: Complex-ellipticity strictly enforces a plane-wave structure on tangent measures.

title: Linear Combination of Hamiltonian Simulation with Commutator Scaling
summary: The Linear Combination of Hamiltonian Simulation (LCHS) framework simulates dissipative linear dynamics by representing time evolution as an integral over unitary operators, which is discretized by quadrature and implemented via Hamiltonian simulation. While existing analyses achieve near-optimal scaling in time and precision using norm-based quantities of the dissipative generator, we show that implementing the Hamiltonian simulation steps with Multi-Product Formulas (MPFs) yields commutator-sensitive error and complexity bounds. We demonstrate that the quadrature rule affects not only discretization error but also commutator structure and query complexity. This dependence is quantified through post-quadrature analysis for abstract MPF error profiles and for general time-independent and local Hamiltonians using known commutator-sensitive MPF error estimates. We compare uniform trapezoidal and free-scale sinh--sinh quadrature, showing improved quadrature-cardinality scaling for the latter, and illustrate the framework with applications to fractional diffusion, advection--diffusion, and open quantum systems.

AI.tldr: This paper improves the LCHS framework for simulating dissipative linear dynamics by using Multi-Product Formulas in Hamiltonian simulation, showing that commutator-sensitive error bounds and advanced quadrature rules (free-scale sinh-sinh) reduce complexity, validated on fractional diffusion, advection-diffusion, and open quantum systems.
AI.motivation: Existing LCHS analyses achieve near-optimal scaling using norm-based quantities, but ignore the impact of commutator structure and quadrature choice on error and complexity.
AI.method: Integrates Multi-Product Formulas (MPFs) into the Hamiltonian simulation steps of LCHS, performs post-quadrature analysis for abstract MPF error profiles and general/local Hamiltonians, and compares uniform trapezoidal vs. free-scale sinh-sinh quadrature.
AI.result: Shows commutator-sensitive error bounds; demonstrates that free-scale sinh-sinh quadrature yields better quadrature-cardinality scaling than uniform trapezoidal; framework validated on fractional diffusion, advection-diffusion, and open quantum systems.
AI.conclusion: The choice of quadrature crucially affects commutator structure and query complexity, and advanced quadrature rules combined with MPFs improve the efficiency of LCHS for dissipative dynamics.

title: Sharp Convergence Rates for Parabolic Green's Functions in Time-Independent Periodic Homogenization
summary: We study Dirichlet Green's functions associated with second-order parabolic systems with rapidly oscillating periodic coefficients that are symmetric and independent of time. For bounded $C^{1,1}$ domains, we obtain a sharp zeroth-order convergence estimate from the oscillating Green's function to its homogenized counterpart, with the optimal rate $O(\varepsilon)$ and Gaussian off-diagonal decay. For bounded $C^{2,1}$ domains, we also prove a first-order expansion for the spatial gradient in terms of Dirichlet correctors, with an $O(\varepsilon)$ error up to a logarithmic factor. In this time-independent symmetric setting, these results improve the convergence rates established by Geng in [Calc. Var. Partial Differ. Equ., 62(6), 2023] for parabolic systems with time-dependent periodic coefficient matrices.

AI.tldr: This paper studies Dirichlet Green's functions for second-order parabolic systems with rapidly oscillating periodic coefficients, proving optimal $O(\varepsilon)$ convergence rates for zeroth-order estimates in $C^{1,1}$ domains and first-order gradient expansions in $C^{2,1}$ domains, improving upon prior work by Geng.
AI.motivation: To establish sharp, optimal convergence rates for Green's functions and their gradients in homogenization of parabolic systems with rapidly oscillating periodic coefficients, improving existing results that had suboptimal rates due to time-dependent coefficients.
AI.method: The paper considers time-independent symmetric periodic coefficients and uses Dirichlet correctors to develop first-order expansions. It analyzes bounded $C^{1,1}$ domains for zeroth-order estimates and $C^{2,1}$ domains for gradient expansions, leveraging the symmetry and time-independence to achieve optimal $O(\varepsilon)$ rates.
AI.result: Sharp $O(\varepsilon)$ convergence rate with Gaussian off-diagonal decay for the zeroth-order Green's function estimate in $C^{1,1}$ domains, and $O(\varepsilon)$ (up to a logarithmic factor) for the first-order gradient expansion in $C^{2,1}$ domains.
AI.conclusion: The time-independent symmetric assumption enables optimal convergence rates that improve upon the previous work by Geng (2023) for parabolic systems with time-dependent coefficients.

title: PHASE: Pauli Hierarchical Assembly on Subdivided Elements for Quantum-Compatible Operator Synthesis
summary: Efficiently decomposing finite element stiffness matrices into the Pauli basis is challenging due to the exponential growth of Pauli strings with problem size. A naive Pauli expansion requires $Θ(8^{\lceil \log_2 N \rceil})$ operations, where $N$ denotes the number of degrees of freedom, rendering direct decomposition infeasible for large systems. Existing approaches exploit algebraic sparsity or operator structure but do not incorporate the geometric organization intrinsic to finite element discretizations, and consequently exhibit poor scaling for stiffness matrices. To address this problem, we introduce PHASE, a hierarchical, geometry-aware Pauli decomposition algorithm that leverages recursive mesh partitioning to organize element contributions across multiple spatial scales. PHASE employs a hybrid strategy that combines full- and reduced-space Tensorized Pauli Decomposition with Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform-based aggregation to assemble global Pauli coefficients efficiently. We show that this approach yields a dimension-dependent reduction in the exponential scaling exponent of Pauli assembly asymptotic complexity relative to existing methods, reducing the cost from $2^{2{\lceil \log_2 N \rceil}}$ to $2^{γ_d{\lceil \log_2 N \rceil}}$ with $γ_d < 2$ under standard mesh regularity and balanced partition assumptions. These results substantially improve the feasibility of quantum-compatible operator synthesis for large-scale finite element models.

AI.tldr: PHASE is a geometry-aware, hierarchical Pauli decomposition algorithm for finite element stiffness matrices that reduces the exponential scaling exponent from 2 to γ_d < 2 by exploiting recursive mesh partitioning and hybrid TPD/FWHT aggregation.
AI.motivation: Naive Pauli expansion requires Θ(8^{⌈log₂ N⌉}) operations, making it infeasible for large finite element systems. Existing methods ignore the geometric structure of finite element discretizations, leading to poor scaling for stiffness matrices.
AI.method: PHASE uses recursive mesh partitioning to organize element contributions hierarchically across spatial scales, combining full- and reduced-space Tensorized Pauli Decomposition (TPD) with Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform (FWHT)-based aggregation for efficient global coefficient assembly.
AI.result: Reduces asymptotic complexity from 2^{2⌈log₂ N⌉} to 2^{γ_d⌈log₂ N⌉} where γ_d < 2, under standard mesh regularity and balanced partition assumptions.
AI.conclusion: PHASE substantially improves the feasibility of quantum-compatible operator synthesis for large-scale finite element models by exploiting geometric structure to lower the exponential scaling exponent.

title: On the almost sure growth of Hölder norms for the 1d periodic fractional BBM equation
summary: We present almost sure polynomial bounds for Hölder norms of solutions of the 1d periodic fractional Benjamin-Bona-Mahony (BBM) equation. Namely, we apply quantitative quasi-invariance of certain Gaussian measures with energy cutoff using the strategy from Tzvetkov (2015) and the globalization argument from Bourgain (1994) in order to extend, almost surely, the $L^2$-based deterministic control to the $L^{\infty}$-based setting.

AI.tldr: The paper proves almost sure polynomial bounds for Hölder norms of solutions to the 1D periodic fractional BBM equation by extending L²-based deterministic control to L∞-based setting using quasi-invariance of Gaussian measures with energy cutoff.
AI.motivation: To establish almost sure polynomial bounds for Hölder norms (L∞-based regularity) for solutions of the fractional BBM equation, going beyond existing L²-based deterministic control.
AI.method: The authors apply quantitative quasi-invariance of certain Gaussian measures with energy cutoff (following Tzvetkov 2015) combined with the globalization argument from Bourgain (1994) to extend L² deterministic estimates to L∞-based Hölder norms almost surely.
AI.result: Almost sure polynomial bounds for Hölder norms of solutions to the 1D periodic fractional BBM equation are obtained.
AI.conclusion: The strategy successfully extends deterministic L² control to higher regularity (L∞-based Hölder) in an almost sure sense, demonstrating robustness of the quasi-invariance approach for fractional BBM equations.

title: A Generalized Framework for $L^r$ Convex Integration and its Application to Geophysical Models
summary:
In this paper a general framework for convex integration is developed, in order to construct weak solutions to the Cauchy problem, by building on ideas from [C. De Lellis and L. Székelyhidi, Arch. Ration. Mech. Anal., 195 (2010)] and [S. Markfelder, Nonlinearity, 37 (2024)]. This framework may be applied to a large family of partial differential equations in order to construct weak solutions in $L^\infty ((0,T) \times Ω)$ (for a bounded domain $Ω)$ which are weakly continuous in time with respect to the weak topology of $L^r (Ω)$ for some $r \in (1,\infty)$. This allows us to construct solutions which obey an energy inequality.
  In the second part of the paper we apply the framework to several inviscid models appearing in the field of geophysical fluid mechanics in order to show existence of weak solutions for all initial data, and to prove that there exist initial data for which there are infinitely many solutions which satisfy an energy inequality. We first consider the incompressible and the barotropic compressible Euler equations to recover the corresponding results from the literature. In addition, the framework allows us to prove a new result for the incompressible Euler equations, namely the global existence for the Cauchy problem in $L^\infty$. Moreover, we use the framework in the context of the hydrostatic Euler equations (also known as the incompressible inviscid primitive equations), which leads to the first convex integration approach which is able to construct admissible solutions with the natural energy for this system. A crucial ingredient in the proof of this result is the computation of a large subset of the convex hull. Finally, we apply the framework to the compressible inviscid primitive equations and to the inviscid quasi-geostrophic equations to obtain the first results on existence of wild data for these two geophysical models.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a general convex integration framework for constructing weak solutions to PDEs, applied to several inviscid geophysical fluid models (Euler equations, hydrostatic Euler equations, primitive equations, quasi-geostrophic equations), proving existence of infinitely many energy-admissible weak solutions for given initial data.
AI.motivation: To establish a unified convex integration framework that can construct weak solutions satisfying energy inequalities across a broad class of PDEs, and to prove existence and non-uniqueness results for various inviscid geophysical fluid models, including previously unaddressed systems like the hydrostatic Euler equations and compressible primitive equations.
AI.method: A general convex integration framework building on De Lellis-Székelyhidi and Markfelder, constructing weak solutions in L∞((0,T)×Ω) that are weakly continuous in time w.r.t. L^r weak topology, with application to incompressible/barotropic compressible Euler, hydrostatic Euler, compressible primitive equations, and inviscid quasi-geostrophic equations.
AI.result: (1) Global existence for incompressible Euler in L∞; (2) First convex integration approach constructing admissible solutions with natural energy for hydrostatic Euler; (3) First wild data existence results for compressible inviscid primitive equations and inviscid quasi-geostrophic equations; (4) Recovery of prior results for Euler equations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed convex integration framework is broadly applicable to many inviscid geophysical models, enabling proofs of existence of infinitely many energy-admissible weak solutions for given initial data, including several first-time results for primitive equations and quasi-geostrophic models.

title: On a continuity method for Dirichlet problem of Hessian equations
comment: 18 pages, to appear in Journal of the Australian Mathematical Society
summary: In this paper, we develop a continuity method for the Dirichlet problem of Hessian equations on Riemannian manifolds. Such equations, introduced by Caffarelli, Nirenberg and Spruck, are defined in terms of the eigenvalues of the Hessian and a given pair $(f,Γ)$, where $f$ is a symmetric function defined in a symmetric cone $Γ\subset\mathbb{R}^n$, and $Γ$ specifies the set of admissible eigenvalues for the solution. Our method combines techniques from Morse theory with a characterization of the pair $(f,Γ)$. More precisely, in the type 2 case, we first construct admissible functions using Morse theory, and then solve the Dirichlet problem without any additional assumptions on the boundary or the subsolution. Building on this characterization of the pair, we can approximate the type 1 equation by a family of type 2 equations.

AI.tldr: Develops a continuity method for solving Dirichlet problems of Hessian equations on Riemannian manifolds by combining Morse theory with a characterization of the admissible pair (f,Γ), enabling solutions for type 2 equations without boundary/subsolution assumptions and approximating type 1 equations via type 2 families.
AI.motivation: To solve Dirichlet problems for Hessian equations on Riemannian manifolds without requiring additional assumptions on the boundary or subsolution, which were previously needed.
AI.method: Combines Morse theory with a characterization of the admissible pair (f,Γ); constructs admissible functions using Morse theory for type 2 case, then approximates type 1 equations by a family of type 2 equations.
AI.result: Successfully solves the Dirichlet problem for type 2 Hessian equations without additional boundary/subsolution assumptions, and provides a way to approximate type 1 equations.
AI.conclusion: The continuity method leveraging Morse theory and pair characterization effectively addresses Hessian equation Dirichlet problems with fewer restrictive assumptions.

title: Accelerated Implicit GDA Schemes: Theoretical Guarantees and Application to Proximal Augmented Lagrangian Methods
summary: Convex optimization problems with linear equality constraints arise ubiquitously in scientific computing, machine learning, and control theory. Classical Krylov methods are effective but rely on problem-specific preconditioners and high memory. Conversely, gradient-based methods like the augmented Lagrangian method (ALM) avoid these issues yet suffer from slow outer iterations. Developing accelerated outer-iteration schemes, therefore, remains a critical research objective. In this study, we demonstrate that incorporating a proximal operation into the augmented Lagrangian framework yields the proximal ALM, where the outer iteration is equivalent to an implicit gradient descent-ascent (GDA) scheme. We further establish that this equivalence extends naturally to the setting of variable step sizes. Through Lyapunov analysis, we show that the underlying potential function must be shifted from the conventional objective gap to a variational inequality measure, signaling a shift in perspective from pure convex optimization to minimax optimization. Motivated by these observations, we first develop an implicit GDA scheme with variable step sizes based on a continuous-time ODE framework, which achieves an $o(1/k)$ last-iterate convergence rate for both the primal-dual objective gap and the gradient norm. Building upon a second-order ODE framework, we then propose a family of Nesterov-type implicit GDA schemes parameterized by $r \geq 0$, which achieves an $o(1/k^{r+1})$ last-iterate convergence rate for the primal-dual objective gap. Furthermore, specializing the second-order ODE formulation to the case $r=0$, we derive a corresponding explicit GDA scheme and prove an $o(1/k)$ last-iterate convergence rate for the primal-dual objective gap. Finally, we present several numerical experiments to validate these theoretical results and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that proximal augmented Lagrangian method is equivalent to implicit gradient descent-ascent (GDA), and develops accelerated GDA schemes (both implicit and explicit) achieving provable last-iterate convergence rates (o(1/k) to o(1/k^{r+1})) for convex optimization with linear equality constraints.
AI.motivation: Classical Krylov methods for convex optimization with linear equality constraints rely on problem-specific preconditioners and high memory, while gradient-based methods like ALM suffer from slow outer iterations. The goal is to develop accelerated outer-iteration schemes.
AI.method: The authors establish equivalence between proximal ALM and implicit GDA. They develop: (1) an implicit GDA with variable step sizes via continuous-time ODE achieving o(1/k) convergence; (2) Nesterov-type implicit GDA via second-order ODE (parameterized by r≥0) achieving o(1/k^{r+1}); (3) an explicit GDA scheme for r=0 with o(1/k) convergence.
AI.result: Implicit GDA: o(1/k) last-iterate rate for primal-dual gap & gradient norm. Nesterov-type implicit GDA: o(1/k^{r+1}) for primal-dual gap. Explicit GDA (r=0): o(1/k) for primal-dual gap. Numerical experiments validate theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: The proximal operation in ALM shifts the perspective from pure convex optimization to minimax optimization (variational inequality measure). The proposed ODE-based GDA frameworks achieve accelerated convergence rates, offering efficient alternatives to classical methods.

title: Stability of the critical constant steady state of a Keller--Segel model
summary: In this paper, we prove the asymptotic stability of the critical constant steady state for a simplified parabolic--elliptic Keller--Segel system in $\mathbb{R}^N$ ($N \ge 3$), which admits a one-parameter family of constant steady states. Although the stability threshold for constant steady states is known, the critical case has remained open. We also show that the convergence rate in the critical case differs from the rates obtained for previously studied subcritical constant steady states.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the asymptotic stability of the critical constant steady state in a simplified parabolic-elliptic Keller-Segel system in $\mathbb{R}^N$ ($N \ge 3$), showing a different convergence rate compared to subcritical cases.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to resolve the open problem of stability for the critical constant steady state in the Keller-Segel system, where the stability threshold was known but the critical case remained unproven.
AI.method: The authors employ mathematical analysis techniques to prove asymptotic stability of the critical constant steady state, and compute the convergence rate behavior for this critical case.
AI.result: The critical constant steady state is asymptotically stable, and the convergence rate in the critical case differs from that of previously studied subcritical constant steady states.
AI.conclusion: The critical case of the Keller-Segel system's constant steady state is now resolved, with a distinct convergence rate behavior that sets it apart from subcritical cases.

title: Adaptive, efficient, and scalable water wave modeling with dispersive hyperbolic systems
summary: Accurate modeling of tsunamis (such as those generated by landslides) requires capturing both wave dispersion in the deep ocean and wave breaking near the shore. The shallow water equations are often preferred for working with tsunamis, but neglect dispersion and may be inaccurate in scenarios where dispersive effects are significant. In this work, we develop an approach that seeks to incorporate the best aspects of both hyperbolic and dispersive models by combining either of two hyperbolic reformulations of the Serre-Green-Naghdi equations away from the shore with the non-dispersive shallow water equations near the shore. The model is discretized and implemented within the GeoClaw software, and incorporates adaptive mesh refinement as well as shared-memory parallelism. We validate it through comparison with benchmarks and real tsunami data. The results and performance compare favorably with the existing dispersive water wave solvers, including a speedup of about 2x relative to GeoClaw's existing dispersive solver for a large-scale tsunami simulation.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a hybrid tsunami model that uses dispersive Serre-Green-Naghdi equations offshore and non-dispersive shallow water equations near shore, implemented in GeoClaw with adaptive mesh refinement and parallelism, achieving ~2x speedup over existing dispersive solvers.
AI.motivation: Accurate tsunami modeling requires capturing both wave dispersion in deep ocean and wave breaking near shore. Shallow water equations neglect dispersion and are inaccurate for dispersive scenarios, motivating a hybrid approach.
AI.method: The approach combines hyperbolic reformulations of the Serre-Green-Naghdi equations (dispersive) away from shore with non-dispersive shallow water equations near shore, discretized in GeoClaw with adaptive mesh refinement and shared-memory parallelism.
AI.result: The model is validated against benchmarks and real tsunami data. It achieves about 2x speedup compared to GeoClaw's existing dispersive solver for large-scale tsunami simulations, with favorable performance versus other dispersive solvers.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid model successfully balances accuracy (dispersion offshore) and efficiency (speed near shore), offering a practical improvement for large-scale tsunami modeling.

title: Schur Visibility and Anti-Phantom Reduction in One-Component Navier-Stokes Degeneration
