summary: We study the finite-scale one-component degeneration problem for suitable weak solutions of the three-dimensional incompressible Navier--Stokes equations under a scale-invariant bound and smallness of the vertical component. Qualitative compactness gives convergence, in the harmonic-pressure quotient, toward the strict two-and-a-half-dimensional boundary, but it does not provide a quantitative rate. This paper proves, in an explicitly abstract trace-obstruction skeleton associated with the old observable closure, that the standard old observable package is insufficient to force a logarithmic or power selected-trace rate. The negative result is an envelope/skeleton theorem, not a Navier--Stokes counterexample. After excluding elementary high-frequency escape by parabolic trace drop and fixed-window analytic obstruction by finite-dimensional Lojasiewicz control, the remaining obstruction is an all-order finite-mode flat branch. We identify the Navier--Stokes-specific mechanism needed to control this branch: strict Schur trace-projectability may fail, but the resulting defect can be visible through the relaxed vertical-pressure channel. In active finite-window models, strict Schur phantoms are relaxed-visible. The final theorem is a conditional dichotomy: either relaxed anti-phantom closure holds and yields conditional logarithmic strict-shadow selection, or there exists an NS-realizable, cleaned, relaxed-invisible, unaligned left-singular cascade.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the finite-scale degeneration problem for Navier-Stokes equations, proving that the standard observable package cannot force logarithmic or power rates. It identifies conditions under which either conditional logarithmic selection holds or a cascade exists.
AI.motivation: To understand why quantitative rates (logarithmic or power) cannot be forced by standard observables in the degeneration problem of Navier-Stokes equations, and to identify the specific Navier-Stokes mechanism needed to control the finite-mode flat branch obstruction.
AI.method: The authors use an abstract trace-obstruction skeleton framework, analyze parabolic trace drop, finite-dimensional Lojasiewicz control, and examine Schur trace-projectability and vertical-pressure channel relaxation. They establish a conditional dichotomy theorem.
AI.result: The standard observable package is insufficient to force logarithmic or power selected-trace rates. The remaining obstruction is an all-order finite-mode flat branch, which can be controlled via relaxed vertical-pressure channel. A conditional dichotomy is established.
AI.conclusion: Either relaxed anti-phantom closure holds (yielding conditional logarithmic strict-shadow selection), or there exists an NS-realizable cleaned cascade that is relaxed-invisible and unaligned.

title: Nearly Instance Optimal Sparse Matrix Approximation from Matrix-Vector Products
summary:
A large body of work studies the problem of learning an approximation to an implicit matrix $A\in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ that is only accessible implicitly via matrix-vector product queries (matvec queries) of the form ${x} \rightarrow {A}{x}$ or ${x} \rightarrow {A}^T{x}$. Of particular interest are methods that learn a near-optimal approximation with a fixed sparsity pattern. For example, we might want to learn a near-optimal diagonal, banded, or arrow-head approximation to an implicit matrix $A$.
  Naturally, the number of matvec queries required to solve this problem depends on the sparsity pattern, which can be encoded as a binary matrix ${S}\in \{0,1\}^{m\times n}$. The query complexity of previous algorithms scales with quantities like the total number of ones in ${S}$, its maximum column/row sparsity, or the chromatic number of a its "conflict graph". These quantities are incomparable: for a given ${S}$, parameterizing by one might yield lower query complexity than another.
  In this work, we unify and tighten these prior results by providing a nearly sharp characterization of the matvec query complexity of sparse matrix approximation. Generalizing a definition from graph algorithms, let the degeneracy, ${degen}({S})$, denote the smallest number $k$ so that, if we iteratively delete all rows and columns of ${S}$ with $\leq k$ ones, we are left with an empty matrix. We show that a near-optimal approximation to $A$ with sparsity pattern $S$ can be learned with $\tilde{O}({degen}({S}))$ matrix-vector product queries, and $Ω({degen}({S}))$ queries are necessary, for any sparsity pattern ${S}$. Moreover, unlike prior work based on graph coloring, all of our methods run in polynomial time.

AI.tldr: This paper characterizes the query complexity for learning sparse matrix approximations via matvec queries, showing that the degeneracy of the sparsity pattern determines the number of queries needed, with near-optimal upper and lower bounds of $\tilde{O}({degen}({S}))$ and $Ω({degen}({S}))$ respectively, while also providing polynomial-time algorithms.
AI.motivation: Prior work on learning sparse approximations to implicit matrices used incomparable complexity measures (e.g., total ones, column/row sparsity, chromatic number of conflict graph), making it unclear which parameterization gives optimal query complexity. The authors aim to unify and tighten these results.
AI.method: The authors introduce the degeneracy of the sparsity pattern matrix $S$, defined as the smallest $k$ such that iteratively deleting rows/columns with $\leq k$ ones leaves an empty matrix. They prove upper bounds ($\tilde{O}({degen}({S}))$ queries) via polynomial-time algorithms and lower bounds ($Ω({degen}({S}))$ queries) via adversarial constructions.
AI.result: The query complexity for learning a near-optimal sparse approximation is $\tilde{\Theta}({degen}({S}))$, providing a nearly tight characterization. Unlike prior graph-coloring-based methods, the proposed algorithms run in polynomial time.
AI.conclusion: Degeneracy is the right measure for matvec query complexity of sparse matrix approximation, unifying and improving prior work while enabling efficient polynomial-time algorithms.

title: Schauder-type Estimates and Log-Critical Well-posedness for the Two-Phase Muskat Problem with Surface Tension
comment: This manuscript corresponds to one part of a study initially published on arXiv (arXiv:2407.05313). The original comprehensive preprint has been divided into a series of papers, each separately addressing the well-posedness of certain free boundary problems, the general case of the Muskat problem, and problems with fixed boundaries. The present article forms Part II of this series
summary:
We prove short-time well-posedness for the Muskat problem with surface tension in the full two-phase setting, allowing different viscosities, arbitrary density contrast, and rigid boundaries. In particular, no Rayleigh--Taylor sign condition on the density contrast is imposed. The interface is assumed to be a graph, uniformly separated from the fixed boundaries, and the initial data may be large in the log-critical class $\dot C^{1,\log^\varkappa}\cap H^1$, with $\varkappa>1$. Thus the result reaches the natural Lipschitz threshold up to a logarithmic correction.
  The main difficulty is that, in the presence of viscosity jump and boundaries, the interface equation is not given by a closed explicit contour dynamics law. Instead, the normal velocity is recovered through an elliptic transmission problem in moving domains, and the resulting evolution is a genuinely nonlocal quasilinear equation. We derive sharp Schauder-type estimates, adapted to the log-critical scale, for the transmission operators generated by the bulk Darcy flow. These estimates identify the third-order parabolic mechanism produced by surface tension and control the nonlinear coupling between the interface geometry and the elliptic transmission structure.
  The proof builds on the Schauder framework developed in Part~I of this series \cite{CHN1}, but requires a new analysis of the Muskat transmission problem in moving domains. Combining this elliptic theory with the contour formulation and time-weighted Hölder estimates, we obtain existence, uniqueness, smoothing, and stability for large interfaces in arbitrary dimension.

AI.tldr: The paper proves short-time well-posedness for the two-phase Muskat problem with surface tension, allowing different viscosities, density contrasts, and boundaries, using Schauder-type estimates and transmission problem analysis in moving domains.
AI.motivation: To establish well-posedness for the Muskat problem with surface tension in the full two-phase setting (different viscosities, arbitrary density contrast, rigid boundaries) without imposing the Rayleigh-Taylor sign condition, and to reach the natural Lipschitz threshold.
AI.method: The approach involves recovering the normal velocity through an elliptic transmission problem in moving domains, deriving sharp Schauder-type estimates adapted to log-critical scales for transmission operators, and combining contour formulation with time-weighted Hölder estimates.
AI.result: Existence, uniqueness, smoothing, and stability for large interfaces in arbitrary dimension are obtained, with initial data in the log-critical class $\dot C^{1,\log^\varkappa}\cap H^1$.
AI.conclusion: The result reaches the natural Lipschitz threshold up to a logarithmic correction, providing a comprehensive well-posedness theory for the two-phase Muskat problem with boundaries and viscosity jumps.

title: Nonlocal Onsager Operators and Entropy Dissipation for Finite-State Schrödinger Bridges
summary:
We investigate the Schrödinger bridge problem on a finite state space with a strictly positive Markov reference kernel. Starting from the semi-dual convex formulation, we introduce a continuous-time evolution for the terminal Schrödinger potential and show that its equilibria coincide with the unique solution of the bridge problem.
  The proposed dynamics induces an evolution for the terminal marginal. This marginal equation is governed by a state-dependent nonlocal Onsager operator, identified with the Hessian of the semi-dual functional. We derive its associated Dirichlet form, establish coercivity estimates on the appropriate quotient space, and interpret the resulting equation as a nonlocal gradient-flow formulation of relative entropy.
  Under natural positivity assumptions, we prove global well-posedness of the SBOF, convergence to the Schrödinger bridge, convergence of the induced couplings and path measures, and exponential relaxation of the terminal marginal. The latter follows from a uniform Poincaré inequality on compact sublevel sets together with entropy--variance comparison estimates. We also discuss the connection with finite-state generative modeling through the Doob transform and illustrate the theory on finite-grid examples involving rare states.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the Schrödinger bridge problem on finite state spaces, introduces a continuous-time evolution (SBOF) for the terminal potential, proves convergence and exponential relaxation, and connects to generative modeling via the Doob transform.
AI.motivation: To analyze the Schrödinger bridge problem through a semi-dual convex formulation and develop a dynamical system perspective with provable convergence guarantees.
AI.method: The authors introduce a continuous-time evolution for the terminal Schrödinger potential from the semi-dual formulation, deriving a nonlocal gradient-flow equation governed by an Onsager operator (Hessian of the semi-dual functional). They use Dirichlet forms, Poincaré inequalities, and entropy-variance estimates.
AI.result: Global well-posedness of the SBOF, convergence to the Schrödinger bridge, convergence of couplings and path measures, and exponential relaxation of the terminal marginal via a uniform Poincaré inequality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dynamics provides a rigorous gradient-flow interpretation of relative entropy for the Schrödinger bridge, with applications to finite-state generative modeling via Doob transform, validated on grid examples with rare states.

title: Martingale Solutions to a Stochastic Keller-Segel System with nonlocal Source and Super-linear Noise
summary: Global nonnegative martingale solutions are shown to exist for a stochastic Keller-Segel system with a nonlocal Fisher-KPP source and super-linear multiplicative noise. The result is obtained for nonnegative initial data with no smallness assumption, provided that the nonlocal source term is dominant. The main difficulty stems from the absence of a coercive structure and the super-linear nature of the noise. An additional cut-off with finite L^2 norm in the classical Galerkin method is added to establish a well-posed approximation problem. Moreover, due to the nonlocal Fisher-KPP structure, it is necessary to prove the positivity of the approximating solution in order to obtain uniform estimates. In the compactness arguments, the usual tightness argument in the framework of Hilbert spaces cannot be directly applied to the uniform estimates obtained in this paper. As a result, we develop a more general version of the compactness argument and tightness criterion, presented in the appendix, which will be applied throughout the paper. This allows for the global existence of nonnegative martingale solutions to be derived from Jakubowski's version of the Skorokhod Theorem, along with a thorough discussion of the convergence properties.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global existence of nonnegative martingale solutions for a stochastic Keller-Segel system with nonlocal Fisher-KPP source and super-linear multiplicative noise, using a novel compactness argument and Galerkin method with cut-off.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to establish global existence of solutions for a stochastic Keller-Segel system under challenging conditions: no coercive structure, super-linear noise, and no smallness assumption on initial data. The nonlocal Fisher-KPP source is required to be dominant.
AI.method: A modified Galerkin method with an additional L^2-norm cut-off is used to ensure a well-posed approximation. Positivity of approximating solutions is proved for uniform estimates. A generalized compactness argument and tightness criterion (developed in the appendix) are employed, combined with Jakubowski's Skorokhod Theorem.
AI.result: Global nonnegative martingale solutions exist for nonnegative initial data, provided the nonlocal source term is dominant. The result holds without smallness assumptions on initial data.
AI.conclusion: The work extends existence theory for stochastic Keller-Segel systems by overcoming the lack of coercivity and super-linear noise through a novel compactness framework, enabling global solutions under mild conditions.

title: Quantum ergodicity and semiclassical measures: mathematical results
comment: Chapter for the Quantum Chaos volume in 'Comprehensive Quantum Mechanics', to be published by Elsevier (Main editor: R.B. Mann; volume editors: S. Gnutzmann and K. {Ż}yczkowski)
summary: In this chapter we review some results describing the high-frequency eigenmodes of the Laplacian on compact manifolds, or Euclidean domains, for which the geodesic flow is chaotic. We focus on the macroscopic distribution of these eigenmodes, which is described by the concept of semiclassical measure. The main result on the question is the Quantum Ergodicity theorem, originally due to Schnirelman. We provide the detailed proof of this theorem, including the adjustments necessary to treat the case of manifolds with boundary. We also discuss the Quantum Unique Ergodicity conjecture, and some progress towards this conjecture for strongly chaotic (Anosov) systems. In particular, we describe the constraints on admissible semiclassical measures, in terms of their Kolmogorov-Sinai entropy, as well as more recent delocalization results.

AI.tldr: This chapter reviews high-frequency eigenmodes of the Laplacian on chaotic manifolds/domains, focusing on semiclassical measures, provides a detailed proof of the Quantum Ergodicity theorem (including boundary cases), and discusses the Quantum Unique Ergodicity conjecture with entropy constraints and delocalization results for Anosov systems.
AI.motivation: To understand the macroscopic distribution of high-frequency eigenmodes of the Laplacian on chaotic systems, which connects quantum chaos to classical geodesic flow.
AI.method: Review and detailed proof of the Quantum Ergodicity theorem (Schnirelman), with adaptations for manifolds with boundary; analysis of semiclassical measures via Kolmogorov-Sinai entropy constraints.
AI.result: Quantum Ergodicity theorem proven in detail; entropy constraints on semiclassical measures for Anosov systems; recent delocalization results discussed.
AI.conclusion: Quantum Ergodicity holds for chaotic systems; the QUE conjecture remains open but progress has been made via entropy methods and delocalization for strongly chaotic systems.
